For more than 2,500 years, the Persian ethnicity and language have defined the core identity of Iranian Civilization. That was not lost on all of the various Europeans who dealt with Iran as an imperial rival, starting from the days of the classical Greeks and continuing to the pagan Romans, the Byzantines, the British, the French, and the Russians. All of them, without exception, always referred to all of Iran and its entire civilizational sphere as “Persia” or the “Persian Empire.” Friedrich Nietzsche wished that it had been the Persians to successfully conquer the Greeks because he believed that they could have gone on to become better guardians of Europe than the Romans proved to be. Nietzsche claimed that “only the Persians have a philosophy of history.” He recognized that historical consciousness, of the Hegelian type, begins with Zarathustra’s future-oriented evolutionary concept of successive historical epochs leading up to an unprecedented end of history.

Iranian Leviathan: A Monumental History of Mithra’s Abode

column4: Significant Figures
Zarathustra, Classical Greeks, Pagan Romans, Byzantines, British, French, Russians, Friedrich Nietzsche
column5: For over 2,500 years, the Persian ethnicity and language have been the foundation of Iranian Civilization. European powers, including the classical Greeks, pagan Romans, Byzantines, British, French, and Russians, consistently recognized and referred to the entire civilizational sphere of Iran as "Persia" or the "Persian Empire," acknowledging its enduring identity. Friedrich Nietzsche held the view that if the Persians had conquered the Greeks, they might have become superior guardians of Europe compared to the Romans. Nietzsche also attributed a unique "philosophy of history" to the Persians, suggesting that a historical consciousness, akin to Hegel's concept, originated with Zarathustra's forward-looking idea of successive historical epochs leading to a final end.

column1: The first revolutionary thesis of this study is that Zoroastrianism can in no way, shape, or form, be considered an uncontested ideological foundation of Iranian Civilization, nor should Zarathustra be seen as the founder of this civilization in anything like the singular sense in which many Chinese would confer upon Confucius the honor of being the father of Chinese Civilization. Rather, if a single religious worldview defined Iranian identity in its formative phase, it was Mithraism, not Zoroastrianism. In Chapter 1, it is argued that Cyrus the Great was not a Zoroastrian, but a worshiper of Mithra and Anahita. Despite the intra-Iranian tribal conflicts between them, the Medes, Persians, Scythians, and Sarmatians were all worshipers of Mithra, the martial god of contracts or covenants, and some form of goddess figure closely associated with him. This goddess figure, variously known as Anahita, Artemis, Satana, or as an arch Gorgon (such as Medusa), was revered as the mother, lover, and initiatrix of Mithra himself or of the Mithraic hero. The opening chapter of this study also discusses how this form of spirituality, in which terrifying sacrifices, ritual intoxication, and the the ordeal by fire, the sacred[…]”
Iranian Leviathan: A Monumental History of Mithra’s Abode

This spiritual tradition involved terrifying sacrifices, ritual intoxication, and the ordeal by fire.

column4: Details & Narrative

Fereydoun (Avestan: Thraetaona) is a hero who defeats the dragon/demon Azi Dahaka (Zahak). He is depicted as a restorer of order, a kingly figure, and a seeker of divine favor for victory and kingship. These stories are preserved in the Avesta and later Pahlavi texts, reflecting Zoroastrian cosmology and mythic kingship15.

Darius I recounts his rise to power, claiming to have overthrown the usurper Gaumata (also called Bardiya), who had seized the throne by impersonating Darius’s predecessor. This foundational inscription is a primary historical source for Achaemenid succession2.
Herodotus records Greek versions of the story of Darius’s accession and the overthrow of Gaumata, blending Persian oral tradition and Greek interpretation2.
Hellanicus, another Greek historian, records versions of the Gaumata/Bardiya affair, further embedding the narrative in Greek historiography2.
Roman-era sources reference versions of the Gaumata/Bardiya story, showing its continued transmission and adaptation in later antiquity.
Darius III, the last Achaemenid king, is reportedly killed by two brothers, Mahyar and Janosyar, as Alexander the Great advances into Persia2.
Narseh ascends the Sasanian throne after a conflict with Wahnam and Warahran, marking a significant episode of dynastic struggle2.
The Paikuli inscription documents King Narseh’s account of his accession and the attempted usurpation, providing valuable evidence of Sasanian royal ideology and succession2.

column2: Composition of the Shahnameh (Book of Kings) by Ferdowsi
column4: Ferdowsi writes the Shahnameh, a monumental Persian epic of over 50,000 couplets, preserving the myths, legends, and history of Iran from the creation of the world to the fall of the Sasanian dynasty. The work draws on earlier prose sources and Pahlavi texts, and is central to Persian cultural identity67910.

column2: Composition of Darab Name by Abu Taher-e Tarsosi
column4: The Darab Name is a prose romance that expands on the legends of Darab (Darius III), blending myth, folklore, and historical motifs. It reflects the continued adaptation of ancient narratives in Islamic-era Persian literature.

column2: Completion of the Shahnameh by Ferdowsi
column4: Ferdowsi completes the Shahnameh, solidifying its place as the national epic of Iran and a cornerstone of Persian literature and identity. The poem is dedicated to Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni after the fall of the Samanids67910.
